text: Why should we trust what Copilot is saying?  I mean as a guess, sure the shared link is a good place to start.  The question is how to check.  And I think there's a simple answer: (1) have an agent write programs in the language with desired output handy, (2) have another instance of the agent try to run them, (3) iterate until the two match.  I use a similar technique to do context engineering.  It works pretty well.

elements.text: Why should we trust what Copilot is saying?  I mean as a guess, sure the shared link is a good place to start.  The question is how to check.  And I think there's a simple answer: (1) have an agent write programs in the language with desired output handy, (2) have another instance of the agent try to run them, (3) iterate until the two match.  I use a similar technique to do context engineering.  It works pretty well.

text: Interesting point. What jumped out at me is the issue of token efficiency - something I hadn’t considered before. As it happens another article was directed at me at about the same time. This <https://felixbarbalet.com/simple-made-inevitable-the-economics-of-language-choice-in-the-llm-era/|one> seems to argue that Clojure is better for LLMs (wrt token efficiency). I’m currently interested in Forth-like and concatenative things like Forth Haiku and advances shown in recent svfig youtubes like this one <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rCF7wAB2wFQ&t=1206s|Stackless Forth> and wondering how that might relate to modern programming and LLM-ery.

attachments.fallback: Context is everything: Simple Made Inevitable: The Economics of Language Choice in the LLM Era
attachments.text:
Two years ago, I wrote about managing twenty microservices at Qantas with a small team. The problem was keeping services in sync, coordinating changes across system boundaries, fighting the entropy of a codebase that grew faster than our ability to reason about it.
